summary: Joel poses a financial puzzle about three government bonds with different terms and interest rates, challenging readers to determine which is the best investment. Bond A pays 4.15% for 10 years, Bond B pays 4.5% for 30 years, and Bond C pays 4.75% with a 10-or-30-year term at the government's discretion. He frames Bond C as the obvious winner since it pays the highest interest rate regardless of duration, then asks readers to think again. The post is a teaser for the next day's answer, structured as a Wall Street literacy exercise.
key_points: The puzzle illustrates that higher coupon rates don't necessarily mean better investments when duration risk and optionality are considered
Bond C contains an embedded call option favoring the government — they choose whether to extend, meaning they'll keep your money longer precisely when it benefits them (when rates drop)
The exercise is designed to teach readers about interest rate risk and the asymmetry of callable bonds
Joel strips away complexity (no taxes, no credit risk) to isolate the core concept of optionality in fixed income
money_quotes: Bond C pays even more! It's 4.75%, w00t.
The point being, the choice is up to the government.
So, it's stupid to buy bonds A or B, right? Bond C is obviously the better choice.
This is not a trick question.
Our summer interns will have some light reading to do before they can get started.
key_insight: When a counterparty holds the option to extend or terminate a deal, a higher headline rate can mask a worse deal — optionality has value, and whoever holds it benefits at the other party's expense.
